data.title: Infosys falls after Q1 and FY27 revenue outlook miss; names CEO designate
data.description:
Shares of Indian technology consultancy firm Infosys (INFY) fell about 4% on Thursday after its fiscal first quarter revenue growth and fiscal full-year 2027 revenue outlook missed estimates. The company also named Ashiss Kumar Dash as CEO designate.

Current CEO Salil Parekh's term ends in March next year.  Dash, who will lead Infosys for the ​next five years, is the head of its ​energy vertical and has been with the company for ⁠more than three decades, according to the company.

In the first quarter ended June 30, the company reported IFRS revenue of about $5.08B, growing 2.8% year-over-year. Meanwhile, EPS jumped about 5% year-over-year to $0.20.

Revenue rose ​14% to 482.11B in Indian rupees but missed analysts' estimates ​of 483.67B rupees, as per data by LSEG, according to Reuters.

"AI momentum is now rapidly converting into revenue, which demonstrates how Infosys’ differentiated enterprise AI value proposition is translating into consistent market share gains. Strong large deal wins, powered by Infosys Topaz, reinforce client confidence in our ability to be the strategic partner of choice for AI transformation driving tangible business value,"  said Parekh.

Total Contract Value, or TCV, of large deal wins was $3.6B, with 61% net new, in the first quarter.

Outlook

The company also ​cut the upper end of its revenue growth forecast to 3% ​from an earlier forecast of 3.5%. It now expects revenue growth of 1.5% to 3% in constant currency for fiscal year 2027.

Analysts had expected a revenue growth estimate of 2.5% to 4.5% for the year, Reuters reported.

Infosys expects an operating margin of 20% to 22% for fiscal year 2027.

Analysts Commentary

Bofa maintained its Buy rating and price target of 1,570 Indian rupees on the stock.

"Q1 revenue growth of 1% qoq, 2.4% yoy (estimated organic growth of 0% qoq) missed our and consensus expectations by 1%. As per company, this was on account of a manufacturing client decision in the quarter alongside the volume and price trends. Management comments in media interview highlighted that the client situation was on account of the deal discussions not making economic sense for the company. New deal wins stood at a reasonable $2.2bn or a 7% yoy increase," said analyst Kunal Tayal and his team.

The analysts noted that management commentary highlighted that deal wins include six consolidation deals in the company's favor — likely suggesting that the client specific impact in the first quarter as not being reflective of the win rate. Al revenues stand at 8.2% of total and has been growing over 10% on a sequential basis, the analysts added.

"Updated revenue guidance of 1.5%-3% is below expectation by about 1% and implies an organic cut of about 1.5% to the top end. Bulk of the cut appears to be driven by the Q1 miss to us," said Tayal and his team.

Well Fargo kept its Equal Weight rating and $11 price target on Infosys stock.

"Expect shares to trade down on disappointing F1Q const-curr growth and lowered F27 revs guide. While reported F1Q was in-line, const-curr growth missed WFSe/Street. Updated F27 const-curr revs guide was also lowered 50 bps at high end despite incremental ~100bps inorganic contribution from Optimum. Newly announced CEO transition (to company veteran Mr. Ashiss Kumar Dash, who takes over 4/1/27 for a 5-year term) makes sense to us given current CEO's term ends 3/31/27," said analysts led by Jason Kupferberg.

Update: The story was updated with analysts' comments and stock price movement.

data.title: Allegion signals 2026 adjusted EPS of $8.85-$9.00 as it raises reported revenue outlook to 7.5%-8.5%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Allegion (ALLE) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "Second quarter results were driven by strong organic growth in the Americas, and we see continued momentum in nonresidential indicators." (President, CEO & Director John Stone)
  • "Our specification activity has been robust for several quarters and includes the breadth of our core institutional markets, cyclical improvement in commercial verticals like office and multifamily and strong growth in data center, which is still small compared to some of our legacy markets, but will continue to gain relevance as that installed base grows and fuels aftermarket over time." (President, CEO & Director Stone)
  • "In our International segment, we made progress on the ERP challenges experienced in the first quarter, consistent with our expectations." (President, CEO & Director Stone)
  • "Demand is weaker in several of our European markets, including Germany, which is our largest market, and we have taken additional restructuring actions in response." (President, CEO & Director Stone)
  • "Revenue for the second quarter was approximately $1.2 billion, an increase of 12.7% compared to last year." (Senior VP & CFO Michael Wagnes)

Outlook

  • "We're raising our reported revenue outlook to 7.5% to 8.5% and our outlook for organic revenue growth to 3.5% to 4.5% based on stronger expected demand in the Americas, partially offset by weaker international demand." (President, CEO & Director John Stone)
  • "We are raising our adjusted EPS outlook to $8.85 to $9.00." (President, CEO & Director Stone)
  • "In the Americas, we're raising our organic assumption to the higher end of mid-single digits, reflecting pricing associated with increased inflation as well as a healthier demand environment, primarily in nonres." (President, CEO & Director Stone)
  • "Our outlook does not include potential IEEPA refunds due to uncertainty on future refund timing and as we prioritize communicating with our customers first." (President, CEO & Director Stone)
  • "For International... weak market demand in Europe, particularly Germany, supports reducing our full year outlook to a low single-digit organic decline." (President, CEO & Director Stone)

Financial Results

  • "Adjusted earnings per share of $2.40 increased $0.36 or 17.6% versus the prior year." (Senior VP & CFO Michael Wagnes)
  • "Q2 adjusted operating margin was 24.2%, up 50 basis points compared to last year." (Senior VP & CFO Wagnes)
  • "Our Americas segment delivered revenue of $918.6 million, which was up 11.8% on a reported basis and up 8.9% on an organic basis." (Senior VP & CFO Wagnes)
  • "Our International segment delivered revenue of $232.9 million, which was up 16.2% on a reported basis, but down 1.2% organically." (Senior VP & CFO Wagnes)
  • "Year-to-date available cash flow was $260.8 million, down 5.3% from the prior year." (Senior VP & CFO Wagnes)

Q&A

  • Timothy Wojs, Robert W. Baird & Co. Incorporated: "what was better relative to your expectations?" Michael Wagnes: "The quarter itself was as strong as I can remember in some time... We did put a price increase out in the market at the end of May... You could have seen a little pull forward as you think of Q3 into Q2... the outlook doesn't assume that level of performance moving forward." (Senior VP & CFO Wagnes)
  • Timothy Wojs, Robert W. Baird & Co. Incorporated: asked for trend/visibility in non-res spec activity into 2027; John Stone: "That strength, that momentum has continued through second quarter. It's as strong as I've seen since I joined the company... specs generally indicating... project work and revenue in the next 12 to 18 months." (President, CEO & Director Stone)
  • Alexander Virgo, Evercore ISI: pressed on Europe vs. a competitor and Germany deterioration; John Stone: "primarily Southern Europe and overweighted in Germany... Germany GDP growth forecast sequentially been taking that down... It's just been the sequential decline in demand in Germany that's had a bit of an outsized impact on us." (President, CEO & Director Stone)
  • Rafe Jadrosich, BofA Securities: asked about resi acceleration and cadence; Michael Wagnes: "inventory levels at retailers are at normal levels... Underlying demand was strong... the outlook doesn't assume that just yet... Q3 last year was particularly strong." (Senior VP & CFO Wagnes)
  • Jeffrey Sprague, Vertical Research Partners: asked about Europe restructuring scope/savings; Michael Wagnes: "think of it as $10 million annually of cost benefit. We'll get the full run rate in Q4." (Senior VP & CFO Wagnes)

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone, concentrated on sustainability of Americas volume/resi strength, Europe demand divergence (Germany), and margin/pricing execution in a weaker international market, including direct challenges like "one of your main competitors last week actually reported accelerating growth in Europe" (Alexander Virgo, Evercore ISI).
  • Slightly positive management tone in prepared remarks around Americas demand signals and raised outlook, with more cautious language in Q&A on residential durability: "the outlook doesn't assume that level of performance moving forward" (Senior VP & CFO Wagnes).
  • Compared with Q1, management messaging shifted from explaining a discrete operational disruption to emphasizing sequential improvement and cost actions: "we made progress on the ERP challenges" (President, CEO & Director John Stone) versus Q1 describing International being "negatively impacted by an ERP implementation" (President, CEO & Director Stone).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 raised full-year targets: "raising our reported revenue outlook to 7.5% to 8.5%" and "raising our adjusted EPS outlook to $8.85 to $9.00" (President, CEO & Director John Stone) versus Q1 "affirming" organic growth and EPS while raising reported revenue to "6% to 8%" to include DCI (President, CEO & Director Stone).
  • The central narrative moved from ERP disruption severity in International in Q1 to recovery and restructuring in Q2: "strong sequential margin improvement" and "additional restructuring actions" (President, CEO & Director Stone).
  • Analyst focus shifted from ERP mechanics and tariff playbook in Q1 to confirming whether Q2 Americas demand included pull-forward, how long spec strength has persisted, and quantifying Europe restructuring benefits.

Risks and Concerns

  • "Demand is weaker in several of our European markets, including Germany" and management response included "additional restructuring actions" and expecting to "build on" sequential improvement in the second half. (President, CEO & Director John Stone)
  • "We announced pricing actions in the quarter to cover the higher inflation we were experiencing, and we'll continue to monitor the tariff and input cost environment to cover additional inflationary pressures if needed." (President, CEO & Director Stone)
  • "The outlook doesn't assume that level of performance moving forward" in residential despite strong Q2 electronics-driven demand. (Senior VP & CFO Michael Wagnes)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q2 as a demand-led acceleration in the Americas with improving nonresidential signals, while acknowledging weaker European demand—especially in Germany—and pairing that softness with restructuring and an ERP recovery plan. Against that backdrop, Allegion raised full-year reported revenue, organic growth, and adjusted EPS outlooks, emphasized pricing actions to cover inflation and tariffs, and reiterated that the share repurchase outlook excludes incremental buybacks despite describing the stock valuation as attractive at current levels.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Oil tops $100 as Houthi attacks reignite Strait of Hormuz fears
data.description:
Brent crude oil (CO1:COM)(CL1:COM) climbed above $100 a barrel for the first time in two months after Iran-backed Houthi forces claimed responsibility for attacks on two Saudi tankers in the Red Sea.

The incidents have heightened confrontations between the United States and Iran, drawing renewed attention to the Strait of Hormuz, one of the world’s most critical energy shipping corridors. 

President Trump stated “the U.S. will hold Iran responsible” for the latest assaults. As regional instability persists, market participants are closely watching when commercial traffic through the strait might normalize. 

Prediction market data from Kalshi indicates that traders assign very low odds to a swift recovery. The probability of traffic returning to normal before August 1, 2026, stands below 1%, rising only modestly to 3% by mid-August and 7% by September 1. 

Odds reach 15% by October 1, 22% by November 1, and 30% by December 1. Even by the start of 2027, the figure is just 38%, climbing to 48% by April 1 and 57% by July 1, 2027. 

The consensus reflected in these contracts underscores expectations that geopolitical risks will continue to constrain shipping flows through the strategic waterway for an extended period. 

Market Tracking ETFs: (DIA), (DDM), (DOG), (DXD), (SDOW), (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (QQQ), (QQQM), (TQQQ), (QID), and (SQQQ).

Oil ETFs: (USO), (UCO), (DBO), (OILK), and (USL).

Energy ETFs: (XLE), (VDE), (XOP), (OIH), (AMLP), and (IXC).

data.title: Quest Diagnostics climbs as Q2 earnings top estimates, guidance raised
data.description:
• Quest Diagnostics (DGX) shares climbed about 11% on Thursday after the diagnostic testing company reported second-quarter results that topped Wall Street estimates and lifted its full-year 2026 guidance above consensus.
  • The company posted adjusted earnings of $3.12 per share, beating analysts' expectations by $0.30, while revenue rose 10.1% year over year to $3.04B, exceeding consensus by $70M.
  • Revenue from the firm’s diagnostic information services surged ~10.3% Y/Y to $2.9B.
  • Looking ahead, Quest Diagnostics now expects full-year 2026 revenue of $11.95B to $12.05B (prior outlook: $11.78B - $11.90B), above the Street estimate of $11.85B. The company also raised its earnings outlook, forecasting adjusted diluted EPS of $11.05 to $11.25 (vs. $10.63 - $10.83 previously), compared with the consensus estimate of $10.77.

data.title: Honeywell Technologies jumps after earnings beat as automation business lifts profit
data.description:
Honeywell Technologies (HON) shares climbed as much as 7.4% on Thursday after the newly streamlined automation company topped Wall Street's second-quarter revenue and profit estimates and raised its full-year earnings outlook, signaling confidence in its post-breakup strategy despite trimming its sales forecast.

The results mark Honeywell Technologies' first earnings report following the June 29 spin-off of Honeywell Aerospace (HONA), transforming the company into a pure-play industrial automation business focused on building, process and industrial automation.

For investors, the separation is intended to simplify the company, sharpen its strategic focus and allow each business to pursue its own capital allocation priorities. Honeywell Aerospace, known for aircraft engines, avionics, auxiliary power units and flight management systems, will report separately beginning next month.

Honeywell Technologies (HON) reported second-quarter sales of $5.19 billion, up 3% from a year earlier and ahead of analysts' consensus estimate of $5.02 billion. Organic sales increased 4%.

Adjusted earnings rose to $1.95 a share from $1.77 a year earlier, topping the Wall Street consensus of $1.82. 

Net income rose to $5.68 billion, or $17.83 a share, from $1.57 billion, or $4.90 a share, a year earlier. The jump reflected a one-time gain from the deconsolidation of quantum computing company Quantinuum (QNT) rather than underlying operating performance.

"The second quarter marked a historic milestone for Honeywell Technologies as we completed the separation of Honeywell Aerospace and began a new era as a leading pure-play automation company," Chairman and Chief Executive Officer Vimal Kapur said.

Orders increased 16% during the quarter, lifting backlog to about $20 billion. Building Automation led the company's businesses with 9% organic growth, supported by demand for fire systems and building services, while Industrial Automation posted 4% organic growth. Process Automation and Technology recorded a 1% organic sales decline as weaker catalyst shipments offset strength in liquefied natural gas projects.

For 2026, Honeywell Technologies (HON) narrowed its sales guidance to $19.8 billion to $20.0 billion from a prior range of $19.9 billion to $20.2 billion. The updated forecast came in below Wall Street expectations of about $20.27 billion.

At the same time, the company improved several profitability targets. It now expects organic sales growth of 3% to 4%, up from 2% to 3%, while raising its adjusted earnings outlook to $8.05 to $8.35 a share from $7.90 to $8.30. The midpoint is slightly above analysts' consensus estimate of $8.23. Honeywell Technologies (HON) also increased its segment margin forecast to 20.1% to 20.5% from 19.8% to 20.3%, while leaving operating cash flow and free cash flow guidance unchanged at about $2.1 billion and $2.0 billion, respectively.

The updated outlook includes the recently completed acquisition of Johnson Matthey's Catalyst Technologies business as well as the expected divestitures of the Productivity Solutions and Services and Warehouse and Workflow Solutions businesses, which are anticipated to close by early August.

data.title: Harley-Davidson Q2 bike sales fall short of expectations
data.description:
Shares of Harley-Davidson (HOG) are under pressure Thursday as the company’s most recent quarterly results were not as bad as Wall Street feared, although motorcycle sales missed expectations despite discounted pricing on some of its more popular models. 

The company shipped 39,200 motorcycles during the quarter, up 9.4% year-over-year, but came in short of Wall Street’s expectations of 40,810.

Additionally, operating income fell 32%, largely due to weakness in its financial services business, while earnings declined 15% year over year to $0.75 per share—still $0.11 ahead of analysts' expectations. The decrease in income was due to a shift in its financial services business ((HDFS)) to a capital-light model, which was only partially offset by improvement in its motorcycle segment ((HDMC)).

Worldwide sales were up 1% as gains in North America (3%) and Latin America (4%) offset flat sales in its Asia Pacific region and a 9% decline in EMEA sales.

LiveWire, the company’s electric motorcycle division, saw a surge in sales, trimming the unit’s loss to $18M versus a loss of $19M in the same quarter last year.

Looking ahead to 2026, HDMC now expects retail sales to be within a range of 133,500 and 138,500 from a prior range of 130,000 and 135,000 units, while operating income is now seen between $10M and $50M from an earlier outlook for a loss of $40M to a profit of $10M.

The company also raised its outlook for HDFS operating income to a range of $55M to $65M from $45M to $60M, previously.

These projections include the assumptions that LiveWire will report an operating loss of $70M to $80M and capital investments of $175M to $200M.

data.title: NovoCure surges on guidance raise, Q2 beats
data.description:
• NovoCure (NVCR) is up ~25% in Thursday morning trading after posting Q2 results that beat on both lines and raising full-year revenue guidance.
  • The oncology company now sees 2026 revenue of $710M-$725M, up from $690M-$710M prior. Consensus is $708.73M.
  • In the quarter, the company's largest Tumor Treating Fields therapy, Optune Gio, saw an 11% increase in active patients to 4,636. Optune Lua patients rose 51% to 207. Also, there were 285 active patients on Optune Pax in the US at the end of the quarter (June 30).
  • NovoCure ended the quarter with cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments of $440.6M compared to $447.7M on Dec. 31, 2025.

data.title: Parnassus Value Equity Fund adds KO; exits BIO
data.description:
• Parnassus Value Equity Fund (PARWX) returned 17.86% in Q2 2026, outperforming the Russell 1000 Value Index, which returned 13.87%, with technology investments leading gains during the quarter.
  • The fund initiated a new position in Coca-Cola (NYSE: KO), citing its strong global brands, pricing power, and long-term growth potential in emerging markets and higher-growth beverage categories.
  • The fund exited its position in Bio-Rad Laboratories (NYSE: BIO), as the stock's risk/reward profile had become less attractive, and redeploying capital into higher-conviction opportunities.
  • Looking ahead, the managers remain cautiously optimistic, expecting quality value stocks to benefit from continued AI infrastructure investment, lower energy prices, and resilient economic conditions while maintaining a focus on quality companies with attractive risk/reward profiles.
  • Source.

data.title: Molina tumbles despite Q2 beat amid high medical expenses
data.description:
Despite reporting better-than-expected financials, Molina Healthcare (MOH) traded lower in the morning hours on Thursday, leading a decline among its managed care peers as analysts focused on its medical costs that exceeded estimates.  

Molina (MOH) fell ~11%, marking its biggest intraday decline since February, while shares of competitors Centene (CNC) and Oscar Health (OSCR), which are yet to report Q2 results, also came under pressure.

On Thursday, Long Beach, California-based Molina (MOH) recorded $1.51 of adjusted EPS for Q2 on $10.9B in revenue, indicating a ~72% YoY and ~5% YoY decline, respectively, but exceeding the consensus by $0.11 and $90M.

The company’s overall medical care ratio reached 92.2% compared to 90.4% in the prior-year period and 92.5% projected by analysts, according to Bloomberg data.

However, its Marketplace business saw a spike in medical costs with MCR reaching 88.9% from 85.4% in Q2 2025 and surpassing 83.9% in the consensus, while membership in the unit also dropped to 230K with nearly a 60% decline from the prior year's period.

Baird analyst Michael Ha, with a Neutral rating on MOH, said headwinds in the Marketplace business remain a matter of concern. “We believe the primary focus of the MOH investment debate continues to be 2027 earnings power,” Bloomberg reported, quoting Ha.

Jefferies analyst David Windley, with a Hold rating, noted multiple issues in the company’s Marketplace business, which he said brings its viability into question. Molina’s (MOH) Marketplace enrollees “also declined slightly more than our estimates, meaning the (40%) organic attrition assumption seems likely, despite the overall market attriting more slowly," the analyst added.

data.title: AppFolio rises as Guggenheim starts with Buy rating
data.description:
• Shares of property management software firm AppFolio (APPF) rose 1.8% in early trading on Thursday as Guggenheim started coverage with a Buy rating and a $232 price target.
  • “We believe the current stock price grossly undervalues the intrinsic value of future cash flows, as it implies overly conservative assumptions in the terminal (or perpetual) period of our DCF,” analyst Tamjid Chowdhury wrote in a note to clients. “We estimate the current valuation implies 0% revenue growth (vs. our assumption of +5%) and/or a significantly impaired FCF margin profile (vs. our assumption of 35%) in the perpetual period. Investor sentiment has soured on the broader application software space, including vertical application software like AppFolio. However, not all verticals are created equal, nor will every software vendor face the same degree of threat from AI. We believe AppFolio is well positioned to, at minimum, preserve its relevance despite the emergence of AI—and potentially capitalize on the opportunity, though the latter remains to be seen.” 
  • Chowdhury continued: “From a numerical standpoint, we believe the company can achieve consensus estimates over the coming years, though upside potential appears more limited in 2026 relative to 2027. The macro backdrop for AppFolio's end market also remains favorable as prolonged elevated interest rates have made renting real estate more economical than owning. That said, recent increases in vacancy rates may partially offset this tailwind. Given these factors, we believe the stock should trade closer to our DCF-derived price target of $232, implying an EV/NTM Recurring Revenue multiple of 7.4x versus the current multiple of approximately 5.1x.”
